entry.title: Washington Post: 5 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: Ukraine scolds Macron; Kyiv’s forces say they have regained ground in east
  • Vigilantes and violence have migrants in South Africa scared for their lives
  • As war drags on, weary Russians yearn for a return to normal life
  • The Queen and Paddington Bear light up a rocking palace
  • Queen Elizabeth misses a favorite horse race, but top hats and fascinators abound
  • A U.S. murder suspect fled to Mexico. The Gringo Hunters were waiting.
  • Milley visits Sweden in show of support for NATO bid
  • Ukraine takes back part of Severodonetsk; revered church engulfed in flames
  • What to know about Severodonetsk, the Ukrainian city Russia wants to capture
  • Tiananmen vigils gain prominence in Taiwan as Chinese threat looms
  • For Hong Kong students, marking Tiananmen may now require ‘hide and seek’
  • At least 28 dead in fire at Bangladesh container depot
  • Philippine volcano spews ash and steam, alarms villagers
  • Pope Francis fuels new speculation on future of pontificate
  • Supreme Leader acknowledges Iran took Greek oil tankers
  • Mystery solved: UK Queen shares secret with Paddington
  • Puerto Ricans speak out on US territory's political status
  • West African leaders put off further post-coup sanctions
  • Albania elects top general as the country’s new president
  • 2 killed as Greek police chase stolen car carrying migrants
  • 8 killed, 15 injured in factory fire in northern India
  • Ex-judge killed in ‘targeted act’ against judicial system, state says
  • Man, 4 grandkids killed by Texas fugitive had gone to ranch to fish
  • 911 dispatcher fired after Tops worker was hung up on during shooting
  • Empty clinics, no calls: The fallout of Oklahoma’s abortion ban
  • Bidens briefly evacuated after plane entered restricted space in Delaware
  • Former Trump adviser Peter Navarro charged with contempt of Congress
  • Economy shows resilience despite mounting recession fears
  • The Long-Lasting Legacy of a Short-Term Prime Minister
  • What About That Anti-Semitic Pig on Martin Luther’s Church?
  • Sheryl Sandberg Is Leaving Facebook at a Perilous Moment
  • Sheryl Sandberg’s ‘Lean In’ Legacy Is Underrated
  • How China Is Reviving Tools for Hedging Credit Risk
  • The Post endorses Kelly Schulz in the GOP primary for Maryland governor
  • Yes, we can do something about gun violence
  • The Medicare and Social Security disaster that Washington is doing nothing to fix
  • Georgetown Law did the right thing on Ilya Shapiro
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more
  • Send a letter to the editor
  • Contact the reader representative
  • Submit an op-ed
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 5 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 5 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-05 07:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • ATM, online banking services in Hong Kong resume for millions of HSBC, Hang Seng customers after brief disruption
  • Record pay rise for civil servants can’t be justified as unemployment soars and the public suffers
  • No cash for coffee? Hong Kong Starbucks in electronic payment-only drive, as industry group slams move as ‘unreasonable’ and consumer watchdog urges transaction choices
  • Publishers warn of grim outlook for industry after 2 more Hong Kong Book Fair rejections
  • ‘How can I bear to let it go?’ As struggling Jumbo Floating Restaurant prepares to exit city, Hongkongers hope for ‘white knights’ to save sinking ship
  • Drastic measures to solve Hong Kong’s housing crisis are long overdue
  • Goodbye ‘Little Thailand’? Kowloon City’s Thai community in Hong Kong reflects on changes to way of life as redevelopment looms
  • Hong Kong police investigate after man drowns off Stanley
  • Hong Kong court sentences 2 former construction firm executives to 4 years in jail each for role in letters of credit scam
  • Hong Kong couple arrested over murder of 14-month-old baby girl, after child found with crescent-shaped marks on neck
  • Coronavirus: Hong Kong nightclub clusters worsen, as commerce chief says opening up of borders must be ‘prudent’ process
  • Why Taiwan fans are cheering Tom Cruise’s return as Top Gun star
  • Chinese aerospace firm files patent for faster, more stable way to launch military drones
  • China declares Shenzhou 14 launch a success as three astronauts lift off to finish Tiangong space station
  • China put the Pacific Islands on the geopolitical map. What comes next?
  • China puts its military to the test in African peacekeeping roles
  • China’s first woman in space Liu Yang returns for Shenzhou 14 mission as next generation takes centre stage
  • Infant among Taiwan’s Covid fatalities as daily death toll reaches new high
  • East Timor deals cap Chinese foreign minister’s 10-day Pacific blitz
  • China’s top e-commerce influencer, ‘Lipstick King’ Austin Li Jiaqi, abruptly ends Taobao show in major live-streaming snafu
  • No autopilot: China says Australia ties reset calls for ‘concrete actions’ from new government
  • One dead in high-speed train crash in southwest China
  • China’s Shenzhou 14 astronaut trio ready for lift-off on Sunday, space agency confirms
  • Vietnam’s child drowning plague, and the community swimming trainers doing all they can to end it
  • North Korea unleashes barrage of missiles in response to US military drills
  • Legalising abortion pill only half the battle for women in Japan
  • ‘Quit your job, live your life’: South Korea’s ‘FIRE tribe’ seek financial freedom in an uncertain world
  • South China Sea: Australia says Chinese jet intercepted surveillance aircraft
  • US-China relations: East Timor isn’t ‘taking sides’ but it wants Beijing’s help, says president
  • American aggression needs to be reined in for the good of Asia and the world
  • South Korea, US stage rare drills with aircraft carrier, amid North Korea nuclear fears
  • At least 10 killed, 22 injured in blast at factory in India that ‘blew the roof off’
  • Indian climber who faked Mt Everest ascent reaches summit for real to ‘prove’ himself
  • Japan considers resuming national tourism discount as Covid eases
  • Indonesian haj pilgrims arrive in Saudi, first since before pandemic
  • Nato chief in push to overcome Turkish opposition to Finland, Sweden joining
  • Explosions rock Ukraine capital Kyiv after weeks of relative calm
  • Elon Musk backtracks on job cuts, says Tesla salaried staff numbers will be little changed
  • Joe Biden delays possible trip to Saudi Arabia and Israel, US media reports
  • Rock band Queen and Paddington Bear kick off Queen Elizabeth’s Platinum Jubilee concert
  • Ukraine says it has recaptured a swathe of the battlefield city of Sievierodonetsk
  • Joe Biden evacuated from holiday home after plane entered airspace near beach house
  • South Asian festival melds with Queen Elizabeth’s Platinum Jubilee in northern England
  • Singer Shakira and footballer Gerard Pique separate after more than a decade together
  • Russian accused of Alexander Litvinenko polonium poisoning dies of coronavirus
  • Zimbabwe vendors battle rumours of human toe-trafficking for witchcraft purposes
  • Oman and Iran sign deals to develop two gas pipelines and an oil field along maritime border
  • Bezos’s Blue Origin flight into space for 5th crewed mission
  • Mariah Carey faces lawsuit over hit song ‘All I Want For Christmas Is You’
  • ‘Cool for active shooter stuff’ – online gun video with 1 million subscribers is just one of many
  • More than 700 monkeypox cases globally, 21 in US, says new report
  • UK police briefly evacuate London’s Trafalgar Square close to Jubilee celebrations site
  • Bill Cosby forcibly kissed me when I was 14, says 61-year-old woman in US court
  • Global struggle against climate change must be fought – and won – in the Asia-Pacific
  • US unicorn Deel wants to ‘revolutionise’ HR function in Hong Kong, help firms hire and pay remote contractors and employees
  • Bank of China, Everbright Bank fined US$2.2 million for flouting asset management rules
  • The history of Hong Kong’s Fire Services Department, and the devastating infernos that forced the city to form a proper response force
  • Cannabis culture blossoms in Chiang Mai, Thailand – in drinks, food, clothing, massages and medicines
  • Eating in the office: whether it’s healthy snacks, junk food munchies, travel souvenirs or free lunches, it says a lot about us
  • He translated Peanuts, wrote lyrics to Astro Boy and is compared to T.S. Eliot – Japanese poetry legend Shuntaro Tanikawa
  • Hong Kong’s first art-house film, Cecille Tong Shu-shuen’s The Arch, starring Crazy Rich Asians’ Lisa Lu Yan, was ahead of its time
  • Laura Dern in Jurassic World Dominion, Tom Cruise in Top Gun: Maverick – 10 actors’ belated returns to iconic movie roles that we love
  • How China’s indie filmmakers struggle to bring the stories – and languages – of rural China to the big screen
  • Why you should spend time outdoors and in nature – it has benefits for your mind and body, happiness and creativity
  • How are NFTs impacting high jewellery investments? Metaverse marketplace OpenSea sells diamonds while Sotheby’s auctioned earrings inspired by a Van Gogh artwork – with accompanying digital token
  • Which Bling Empire cast member has the most fabulous home? From Mimi Morris’ US$9 million California mansion and Kim Lee’s Malibu beach pad, to Jaime Xie’s West Hollywood penthouse
  • The fabulous life of Dream Kardashian: Rob and Blac Chyna’s daughter will earn US$50,000 for an Amazon advert with Kris Jenner, owns a baby Bentley and lives in a US$9 million mansion
  • Who is the richest woman in the world in 2022? Net worths, ranked: Jeff Bezos’ billionaire ex wife MacKenzie Scott ranks at No 4, but is eclipsed by Walmart’s Alice Walton and L’Oreal’s Françoise Bettencourt Meyers
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: Guardian: 5 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Sheryl Sandberg’s influence reaches all of us. But it’s a troubling legacy | Stephanie Hare
  • Giving up the rules for a hot summer body
  • At least 34 killed and scores injured in Bangladesh depot fire

  • Nadal wishes for new foot but stands a match away from 14th French Open
  • Iga Swiatek reigns at French Open again after swatting Coco Gauff aside
  • Elif Shafak: there’s a scream building up in young people
  • How Texas boys’ dream trip ended in family massacre at hands of fugitive
  • Alexander Litvinenko assassination suspect dies of Covid
  • Enemy tongue: eastern Ukrainians reject their Russian birth language
  • JD Vance is firmly against banning guns – but he’s keen on banning porn | Arwa Mahdawi
  • Indira Varma: ‘I wanted to be a clown. Or David Attenborough’
  • Shower in your bra and cook in the kettle? Putting ‘extreme frugality’ to the test
  • Deborah James describes anger and fear over dying of bowel cancer
  • John Waters: ‘Trump ruined bad taste – he was the nail in the coffin’
  • Antony Gormley to become German citizen due to ‘tragedy’ of Brexit

The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.

  • What The Matrix Got Wrong About Cities of the Future
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

  • A down-to-earth approach to climate change
  • Loops simplify a set-up to boost quantum computational advantage
  • Nerve regrowth can be painful
  • The 6G frequency switch that spares scientific services
  • Clues to the identity of the fossil fish Palaeospondylus
  • From the archive: international efforts to curb pollution, and the perils of broadcasting
  • Multilayer 2D insulator shows promise for post-silicon electronics
  • An immune molecule segregates memories in time
  • The road to fully programmable protein catalysis
  • Strongly correlated electron–photon systems
  • Resolving the H i in damped Lyman α systems that power star formation
  • Probing CP symmetry and weak phases with entangled double-strange baryons
  • Magneto-optical trapping and sub-Doppler cooling of a polyatomic molecule
  • Quantum computational advantage with a programmable photonic processor
  • Non-Hermitian chiral phononics through optomechanically induced squeezing
  • Epitaxial single-crystal hexagonal boron nitride multilayers on Ni (111)
  • A tissue-like neurotransmitter sensor for the brain and gut
  • [18F]Difluorocarbene for positron emission tomography
  • Morphology of Palaeospondylus shows affinity to tetrapod ancestors
  • The mosaic oat genome gives insights into a uniquely healthy cereal crop
  • Single-cell eQTL models reveal dynamic T cell state dependence of disease loci
  • People construct simplified mental representations to plan
  • Neuropathic pain caused by miswiring and abnormal end organ targeting
  • CCR5 closes the temporal window for memory linking
  • Gene regulation by gonadal hormone receptors underlies brain sex differences
  • Distinct gene clusters drive formation of ferrosome organelles in bacteria
  • Developmental dynamics of two bipotent thymic epithelial progenitor types
  • Fundamental immune–oncogenicity trade-offs define driver mutation fitness
  • Mitochondrial uncouplers induce proton leak by activating AAC and UCP1
  • Gibbin mesodermal regulation patterns epithelial development
  • MCM complexes are barriers that restrict cohesin-mediated loop extrusion
  • Fast and efficient DNA replication with purified human proteins
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: New Yorker: June 6, 2022

Articles in this issue:

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 2 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 2 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-02 23:40PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “Thank You, Mr Nixon” by Gish Jen
  • Podcast with Erin Murphy, author of “Burmese Haze: US Policy and Myanmar’s Opening—and Closing”
  • New Book Announcement: “Water Thicker Than Blood: A Memoir of a Post-Internment Childhood” by George Uba
  • “Picking Off New Shoots Will Not Stop the Spring”, edited by Ko Ko Thett and Brian Haman
  • Opera in brief: Musica Viva’s abridged Giulio Cesare
  • New Book Announcement: “The Substance of Fiction: Literary Objects in China, 1550–1775” by Sophie Volpp
  • “I Was the President Mistress!!” by Miguel Syjuco
  • New Book Announcement: “Ethical Encounters: Transnational Feminism, Human Rights, and War Cinema in Bangladesh” by Elora Halim Chowdhury
  • “In the Forest of the Blind: The Eurasian Journey of Faxian’s Record of Buddhist Kingdoms” by Matthew W King
  • “Solo Dance” by Li Kotomi
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

entry.title: London Review of Books: 9 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: London Review of Books: 9 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-01 13:41PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
